preamble: The shift from the voiceless velar stop [k] to the voiceless uvular stop [q] is a process of retraction or uvularization. It typically occurs in environments with back vowels or retracted consonants, or as part of a push-chain movement to maintain phonetic distance from newly developed fronted sounds like [c] or [tʃ].

examples.note: Retraction of velar stops in the presence of emphatic consonants or specific back-vowel environments.
Historical retraction often driven by the development of palatalized series pushing the original velars further back.
Synchronic retraction: the [k] in 'cool' is articulated significantly further back than in 'keep' due to the following back vowel [u].

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Watson, J. C. (2002). The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic.
